name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ لِّلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ وَزِيَادَةٌ ۖ وَلَا يَرْهَقُ وُجُوهَهُمْ قَتَرٌ وَلَا ذِلَّةٌ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٦)
```

```english
Those who are virtuous shall receive the best reward and an enhancement. Neither dust nor abasement shall overcast their faces. They shall be the inhabitants of paradise, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (26)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَسَبُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ جَزَآءُ سَيِّئَةٍۭ بِمِثْلِهَا وَتَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ۖ مَّا لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ ۖ كَأَنَّمَآ أُغْشِيَتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ قِطَعًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ مُظْلِمًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٧)
```

```english
For those who have committed misdeeds, the requital of a misdeed shall be its like, and they shall be overcast by abasement. They shall have no one to protect [them] from Allah. [They will be] as if their faces were covered with dark patches of the night. They shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (27)
```

```arabic
وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ مَكَانَكُمْ أَنتُمْ وَشُرَكَآؤُكُمْ ۚ فَزَيَّلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ وَقَالَ شُرَكَآؤُهُم مَّا كُنتُمْ إِيَّانَا تَعْبُدُونَ (٢٨)
```

```english
On the day when We gather them all together, We shall say to those who ascribe partners [to Allah], ‘Stay where you are—you and your partners!’ Then We shall set them apart from one another, and their partners will say, ‘It was not us that you worshipped. (28)
```

```arabic
فَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمْ إِن كُنَّا عَنْ عِبَادَتِكُمْ لَغَـٰفِلِينَ (٢٩)
```

```english
Allah suffices as a witness between you and us. We were indeed unaware of your worship.’ (29)
```

```arabic
هُنَالِكَ تَبْلُوا۟ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّآ أَسْلَفَتْ ۚ وَرُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَوْلَىٰهُمُ ٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٣٠)
```

```english
There every soul will examine what it has sent in advance, and they will be returned to Allah, their real master, and what they used to fabricate will forsake them. (30)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a new discourse returning to the mention of the recompense for deeds and the return of all to Allah, the Truth (الحق). An allusion to this has already preceded, and within it is the establishment of the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His creation, sovereignty, and governance of the universe*.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا الْحُسْنَى وَزِيَادَةٌ وَلَا يَرْهَقُ وُجُوهَهُمْ قَتَرٌ وَلَا ذِلَّةٌ** “For those who do good is the best reward, and an extra. Neither dust nor abasement shall overcast their faces.” (10:26). *Al-Husna* (الحسنى) is the feminine form of *ahsan* (أحسن), and what is meant is the best reward (المثوبة الحسنى). What is meant by "extra" (الزيادة) is that which exceeds what is deserved, based on the fact that Allah, out of His grace (فضل), has made for every deed a certain measure of recompense and reward, then made it a right for the worker, as in His saying: **لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ** “they shall have their reward near their Lord” (3:199). Then He multiplied it and made the multiplied portion also a right for the worker, as in His saying: **مَنْ جَاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثَالِهَا** “Whoever brings a good deed shall receive ten times its like” (6:160). At that point, the import of His saying, “For those who do good is the best reward,” is their entitlement to the best recompense and reward, and the "extra" would be the increase over the amount deserved—whether the single like or the ten likes—similar to the meaning conveyed by His saying: **فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَيَزِيدُهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ** “As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, He will pay them their rewards in full, and enhance them out of His grace.” (4:173).

If what is meant by *al-Husna* in His saying, “For those who do good is the best reward,” were the "best end" (العاقبة الحسنى), and since there is nothing intellectually conceivable above the "best," then the meaning of His saying, “and an extra,” would be an increase over what a human can conceive of divine grace (الفضل الإلهي). This is indicated by His saying: **فَلَا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ مَا أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ** “No one knows what has been kept restorative for them” (32:17), and what is in His saying: **لَهُمْ مَا يَشَاءُونَ فِيهَا وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ** “There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more.” (40:35). For it is known that every good thing a human desires is surpassed by the "more" (المزيد) which is beyond what he can perceive. So understand that.

The term *al-rahaq* (الرهق) refers to overtaking or covering. It is said: "Debt overtook (*rahaqahu*) him," meaning it caught up to him and covered him. *Al-qatar* (القتر) is black smoke or black dust. Describing them with His saying, “Neither dust nor abasement shall overcast their faces,” is a parallel to the description of the people of the Fire in the following verse regarding the blackness of their faces with *qatar*—which is a physical blackness—and abasement (الذلة)—which is a spiritual blackness.

The meaning is: For those who did good in the world is the best reward and an extra from the grace of Allah—or the best end and an extra that does not cross their minds—and no blackness from dust or abasement shall cover their faces; “those are the inhabitants of paradise; they shall remain in it [forever].”

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ كَسَبُوا السَّيِّئَاتِ جَزَاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ بِمِثْلِهَا وَتَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ** “As for those who have committed vices, the requital of a vice shall be its like, and they shall be overcast by abasement.” (10:27) to the end of the verse. The phrase “the requital of a vice shall be its like” (جَزَاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ بِمِثْلِهَا) is a subject (مبتدأ) for an omitted predicate (خبر), the estimation being: "For them is the requital of a vice with its like of punishment." This entire sentence is the predicate for the subject which is His saying, “As for those who have committed vices.” The meaning is that those who earned vices are not recompensed except with the like of the evil punishments they committed; thus, the requital for an evil act is an evil punishment.

His saying: **مَا لَهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ** “They shall have no one to protect them from Allah” means they have no protector to shield them from Allah, i.e., from His punishment. In this is a negation of their partners (شركائهم) whom they imagine to be intercessors (شفعاء), in a manner that negates every preventing protector, whether it be a partner-intercessor, a powerful opposing preventer, or any protector other than them.

His saying: **كَأَنَّمَا أُغْشِيَتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ قِطَعًا مِنَ اللَّيْلِ مُظْلِمًا** “as if their faces were covered by layers of the dark night” (10:27). *Qita‘an* (قطعاً) is the plural of *qit‘ah* (قطعة) — *a piece or segment*, and *muthliman* (مظلماً) — *dark* is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for "the night." The meaning is as if the dark night were divided into segments and those segments were cast over their faces, making them completely black. The immediate sense is that the face of each of the polytheists is covered by one of those segments, not as some have interpreted it to mean that the faces were covered by those segments one after another, resulting in layers of darkness one above the other; for there is nothing in the speech to indicate that.

His saying: **أُولَئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ** “They shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they shall remain in it [forever]” indicates the permanence of their stay in the Fire, as the terms "inmates" (أصحاب) and "immortality" (الخلود) denote this, just as its counterpart regarding the inhabitants of Paradise indicates the same for them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا مَكانَكُمْ أَنْتُمْ وَشُرَكاؤُكُمْ** “On the day We shall muster them all together, then We shall say to the polytheists, ‘Stay in your place, you and your partners!’” (10:28) to the end of the verse. What is meant is the gathering (حشر) of all those previously mentioned—the believers, the polytheists, and their partners. For He, the Exalted, mentions the polytheists and their partners in this verse and the one following it, then refers to everyone by His saying in the subsequent verse: **هُنالِكَ تَبْلُوا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما أَسْلَفَتْ** “There every soul will examine what it has sent in advance.”

His saying: **ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا مَكانَكُمْ أَنْتُمْ وَشُرَكاؤُكُمْ** “then We shall say to the polytheists, ‘Stay in your place, you and your partners!’” means: "Remain in your place, you, and let your partners remain in their place." Following this address, "We shall set them apart from one another" (فَزَيَّلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ), severing the bond that used to tie them to their partners—which was the bond of illusion (الوهم) and assumption (الحسبان) through which they connected to their partners. Thus, they were cut off from their partners and their partners from them, and it became clear that their worship (عبادتهم) did not actually fall upon them nor relate to them, because they only worshipped "partners," and those were not truly partners.

The evidence for what we have mentioned is His saying, the Exalted, immediately after: **وَقالَ شُرَكاؤُهُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ إِيَّانا تَعْبُدُونَ** “and their partners will say, ‘It was not us that you used to worship.’” (10:28). The speech is to be taken according to its literal sense as a serious and truthful negation of their worship of them. They are not lying in this speech, as evidenced by their reliance on the testimony of Allah, the Glorified. Nor do they mean "we did not call you to worship us," for the wording does not suit this meaning. Nor is their intention to taunt them by saying "you were actually worshipping your own desires and the satans who misled you," for that does not suit their claim of being unaware (الغفلة). Likewise, it is not suited by His saying afterward: **هُنالِكَ تَبْلُوا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما أَسْلَفَتْ** “There every soul will examine what it has sent in advance,” according to the meaning that will follow. Rather, their intention is to negate the worship in reality by negating the reality of the partnership (الشركة), and citing as evidence for that the testimony of Allah and His knowledge of their unawareness of their worship.

Worship (العبادة) — *the act of ultimate submission and devotion* — which is a connection involving being owned (المملوكية) and humility (التذلل) from the worshipper toward the worshipped, only becomes worship if it connects and links to the worshipped—so that the meaning of the preposition "for" (اللام) is fulfilled in our saying: "worship is *for* him." This cannot occur except with the awareness (شعور) of the worshipped and his knowledge of it. If knowledge is not realized there, then worship is not realized in reality; it is merely the form of worship (صورة عبادة).

It has thus become clear that the meaning of His saying, “then We shall say to the polytheists, ‘Stay in your place, you and your partners!’ then We shall set them apart from one another,” is His manifestation and disclosure, the Exalted, on that day of the reality of the matter which illusions had covered and desires had veiled in the world. That reality is that the true mastership (المولوية) and the ownership of the realm of governance (مالكية زمان التدبير) belong to Allah, the Glorified, and nothing else possesses any mastership or lordship (ربوبية) such that seeking refuge in it would be valid or worship of it would be true.

When Allah removes the veil from the face of this reality on that day, it will become clear to the polytheists that their partners were not truly partners nor were they worshipped by them in reality—due to their unawareness of their worship—and that they were only performing for them a form of worship which illusion and desire depicted as being worship, when it was not.

To this also points the saying of the Exalted: **وَإِذَا رَأَى الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا شُرَكاءَهُمْ قالُوا رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ شُرَكاؤُنَا الَّذِينَ كُنَّا نَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِكَ فَأَلْقَوْا إِلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلَ إِنَّكُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ** “When the polytheists see their partners, they will say, ‘Our Lord! These are our partners whom we used to invoke besides You.’ But they will retort at them, ‘You are indeed liars!’” (16:86).

It has also become clear from this that His saying, “and their partners will say, ‘It was not us that you used to worship,’” is a statement from their partners to them in earnest and truth. This reveals the error of the view of some who say: "The meaning is: 'You did not worship us by our command or our call,' not that you did not worship us at all, because that would be a lie, and lying is not permitted to occur in the Hereafter because they are compelled therein to abandon what is foul."

For the negation of the essence of worship, according to the meaning you have come to know, is the absolute truth. Affirming the worship, while not a lie, is not free from a degree of metaphor (مجاز) when considering the reality of the matter. Furthermore, what he mentioned—that the meaning is the negation of worship by their command and call—is a meaning for which there is no evidence from the wording.

Moreover, lying only does not occur in the Hereafter if it is an act and an acquisition (كسبا); as for it being a result of worldly dispositions (الملكات), there is no barrier to its possibility. In fact, it does occur, as He, the Exalted, narrates in His saying: **ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُنْ فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا وَاللَّهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ انْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ** “Then their only excuse will be to say, ‘By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists.’ See how they lie against themselves” (6:23-24), and other verses.

Similarly, the view of some who say: "The meaning is: 'You did not exclusively worship us; rather, you were worshipping your desires, your lusts, and your satans who misled you.'" For the fact that worshipping desires and Satan is true of their action—from the perspective that it is a following of desire and Satan—does not negate the fact that it is also truly a worship of idols. Allah affirms all three perspectives in His speech; He, the Exalted, says: **وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ ما لا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلا يَنْفَعُهُمْ** “They worship besides Allah that which neither harms them nor benefits them” (10:18); and He says: **أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلَهَهُ هَواهُ** “Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god?” (45:23); and He says: **أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ** “that you should not worship Satan? He is indeed your manifest enemy.” (36:60).

It is well known that the partners are arguing to negate their being worshipped by them, not to prove that desire and Satan were worshipped along with the partners; for this would not benefit them in the argument at all, and it would render their affirmation of unawareness for themselves futile in their saying: **إِنْ كُنَّا عَنْ عِبادَتِكُمْ لَغافِلِينَ** “we were indeed unaware of your worship.” (10:29). This is because desires also were not conscious of their worship, just as the idols, being dead bodies, were not.

Perhaps the proponent of this view relied on the restriction (الحصر) understood from His saying: **ما كُنْتُمْ إِيَّانا تَعْبُدُونَ** “It was not us that you used to worship,” by placing the object before its verb. Its literal sense is a "reversal restriction" (قصر قلب) whose import is the negation of being worshipped from themselves and its affirmation for others, not a negation of the essence of worship; for they affirm it in their saying: “of your worship” (عَنْ عِبادَتِكُمْ), as the addition of the verbal noun (المصدر) to its object implies its existence.

However, the truth is that these partners only said to them, “It was not us that you used to worship,” in response to what the polytheists said, as narrated by Allah: **رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ شُرَكاؤُنَا الَّذِينَ كُنَّا نَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِكَ** “Our Lord! These are our partners whom we used to invoke besides You.” (16:86). Thus, they negated their worship of Allah, the Glorified, and affirmed it for the partners. The partners had no interest except to negate the worship of the polytheists from themselves. As for whom it was affirmed for, they had no purpose related to that; rather, their concern was to disassociate themselves from the claim of partnership. They argued for this by affirming unawareness (الغفلة) of that for themselves; for if they had been conscious of their worship and had accepted them as worshippers, it would have necessitated that they—the partners—were claiming partnership.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَكَفى بِاللَّهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمْ** “Allah suffices as a witness between us and you” (10:29) to the end of the verse. Its meaning has become clear through the preceding explanation. The *fa* (ف) in His saying, “So Allah suffices” (فَكَفى بِاللَّهِ), provides the justification (التعليل), like our saying: "Worship Allah, for He is your Lord," and this is common in speech.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هُنالِكَ تَبْلُوا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما أَسْلَفَتْ** “There every soul will examine what it has sent in advance” (10:30) to the end of the verse. *Al-bala’* (البلاء) is testing or examination (الاختبار). The indication in His saying, “There” (هُنالِكَ), is to the station (الموقف) He mentioned by His saying: “then We shall say to the polytheists, ‘Stay in your place, you and your partners!’ then We shall set them apart from one another.”

That station is a station where every soul will test and examine what it has sent in advance and performed of deeds. The reality of its deeds will be disclosed to it, and it will witness them with its own eyes—not merely through memory or description. By witnessing the truth (الحق) of everything directly, it will be revealed that the True Master (المولى الحق) is Allah, the Glorified. All illusions will fall and crumble, and all the claims that man fabricated through his illusions and desires against the Truth will vanish.

These fabrications and claims only arose from the connections we establish in this world between causes and effects, and the independence and mastership we grant to causes. Yet there is no god but Allah, and no true master but He, the Glorified. When the reality of the matter is unveiled, the cloud of illusion is dispersed, and the veil of claims is torn, it will appear that there is no true master but He, the Glorified. All the deities that were only established by human fabrication will be nullified, and all deeds will fall and be rendered void, except those through which Allah, the Glorified, was worshipped with true worship.

Thus, the three segments of the verse—namely His saying, “every soul will examine...”, His saying, “they shall be returned to Allah...”, and His saying, “and that which they used to fabricate will forsake them”—each helps the others in conveying the reality of its meaning. The sum total of their import is the manifestation of the reality of Divine Guardianship (الولاية الإلهية) on that day as a direct witnessing, and that nothing besides Him, the Exalted, possesses anything but pure poverty and being owned. At that point, every false claim will be nullified and the edifice of illusions will be demolished.

As indicated by His saying: **هُنالِكَ الْوَلايَةُ لِلَّهِ الْحَقِّ** “There, guardianship belongs only to Allah, the Real.” (18:44); and His saying: **يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى عَلَى اللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ** “The day when they will emerge [from their graves], nothing about them will be hidden from Allah. ‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’” (40:16); and His saying: **وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ** “and on that day all command will belong to Allah.” (82:19), among others.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Amali al-Mufid*, with its chain to Abu Ishaq al-Hamdani from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): In what he wrote to Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr when he appointed him over Egypt—and commanded him to read it to the people, and in what he wrote: Allah the Exalted said: **لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا الْحُسْنَى وَزِيَادَةٌ** — and *al-Husna* is Paradise, and the "extra" is the world.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "As for *al-Husna*, it is Paradise; and as for the 'extra', it is the world—what Allah gave them in the world, Allah will not hold them to account for in the Hereafter, and Allah will gather for them the reward of the world and the Hereafter." (The tradition).

**I say:** The two narrations are looking at the first meaning we presented in the preceding commentary. Al-Tabarsi narrated something with the meaning of the second in *al-Majma‘* from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him).

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, it is narrated in *Nahj al-Bayan* from Ali ibn Ibrahim that he said: "The 'extra' is the gift of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Daraqutni and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Suhayb regarding the verse: He said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: "The 'extra' is looking at the face of Allah."

**I say:** This meaning is narrated through several chains from the sources of the Sunnis from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny). The clarification of its meaning has already preceded in the commentary on the saying of the Exalted: **رَبِّ أَرِنِي أَنْظُرْ إِلَيْكَ** “My Lord, show [Yourself] to me, that I may look at You!” (7:143) in the eighth volume of this book.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): Regarding His saying: **كَأَنَّما أُغْشِيَتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ قِطَعاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ مُظْلِماً** — he said: "Do you not see the house when it is night—it is more intensely black than the outside—likewise their faces increase in blackness."

**I say:** Al-Ayyashi narrated it from Abu Basir from him (peace be upon him). It is as if he (peace be upon him) intends to interpret the "segments of the night" (القطع من الليل) mentioned in the verse.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu al-Shaykh extracted from al-Suddi: Regarding His saying: **وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللَّهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ** — he said: "It was abrogated by His saying: **مَوْلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَأَنَّ الْكافِرِينَ لا مَوْلى لَهُمْ** ('[That is] because Allah is the protector of the faithful, and because the faithless have no protector')."

**I say:** This is among the most absurd of statements. Rather, the two verses look at two different aspects of meaning, which are the apparent (الظاهر) and the hidden (الباطن).
